verses.explainer: The soldiers take Jesus into the governor's headquarters and gather the whole battalion -- 200-600 soldiers -- around him.

verses.text: They stripped him, and put a scarlet robe on him.
verses.explainer: They strip him and put a scarlet robe on him -- a mock royal garment.

verses.text: They braided a crown of thorns and put it on his head, and a reed in his right hand; and they kneeled down before him, and mocked him, saying, “Hail, King of the Jews!”
verses.explainer: They twist together a crown of thorns and put it on his head, and a reed in his right hand (a mock scepter). They kneel before him and mock: 'Hail, King of the Jews!' Everything about kingship is parodied.

verses.text: They spat on him, and took the reed and struck him on the head.
verses.explainer: They spit on him, take the reed, and strike him on the head. The thorns are driven deeper with each blow.

verses.text: When they had mocked him, they took the robe off him, and put his clothes on him, and led him away to crucify him.
verses.explainer: After mocking him, they strip the robe off and put his own clothes back on. They lead him away to crucify him.

verses.text: As they came out, they found a man of Cyrene, Simon by name, and they compelled him to go with them, that he might carry his cross.
verses.explainer: They force a man named Simon of Cyrene to carry Jesus' cross. Jesus is too weakened from flogging to carry it himself. Simon was from North Africa -- a random bystander drafted into history.

verses.text: When they came to a place called “Golgotha”, that is to say, “The place of a skull,”
verses.explainer: They come to Golgotha, which means 'Place of a Skull.' The execution site outside Jerusalem's walls.

verses.text: they gave him sour wine to drink mixed with gall. When he had tasted it, he would not drink.
verses.explainer: They offer him wine mixed with gall to drink. A mild painkiller. He tastes it but refuses. He will experience the full weight of the cross without numbing.

verses.text: When they had crucified him, they divided his clothing among them, casting lots,
verses.explainer: They crucify him and divide his garments by casting lots. Crucifixion involved being nailed to a wooden cross and left to die slowly from exhaustion and suffocation. Matthew quotes Psalm 22:18 -- even the gambling for his clothes was prophesied.

verses.text: and they sat and watched him there.
verses.explainer: They sit down and keep watch over him there. Guards assigned to wait for death.

verses.text: They set up over his head the accusation against him written, “THIS IS JESUS, THE KING OF THE JEWS.”
verses.explainer: Over his head they place the written charge: THIS IS JESUS, THE KING OF THE JEWS. Intended as mockery, it is the most accurate sign in the world.

verses.text: Then there were two robbers crucified with him, one on his right hand and one on the left.
verses.explainer: Two robbers are crucified with him, one on the right and one on the left. The positions James and John's mother asked for (chapter 20) are filled by criminals.

verses.text: Those who passed by blasphemed him, wagging their heads,
verses.explainer: Those passing by hurl insults, shaking their heads.

verses.text: and saying, “You who destroy the temple, and build it in three days, save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down from the cross!”
verses.explainer: You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down from the cross! The same challenge the devil posed in the wilderness: prove yourself. Use your power for yourself.

verses.text: Likewise the chief priests also mocking, with the scribes, the Pharisees, and the elders, said,
verses.explainer: The chief priests, scribes, and elders mock him. Even the religious leaders come to gloat.

verses.text: “He saved others, but he can’t save himself. If he is the King of Israel, let him come down from the cross now, and we will believe in him.
verses.explainer: He saved others; he cannot save himself. If he is the King of Israel, let him come down from the cross and we will believe him. The deepest irony: it is precisely because he will not save himself that he saves others.

verses.text: He trusts in God. Let God deliver him now, if he wants him; for he said, ‘I am the Son of God.’”
verses.explainer: He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him. For he said, 'I am the Son of God.' They unknowingly echo Psalm 22:8 -- another prophecy fulfilled by their very mockery.

verses.text: The robbers also who were crucified with him cast on him the same reproach.
verses.explainer: The robbers crucified with him also revile him. He is rejected from every direction: leaders, crowds, fellow condemned.

verses.text: Now from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour.
verses.explainer: From noon until three in the afternoon, darkness covers the whole land. Three hours of supernatural darkness. Creation itself recoils.

verses.text: About the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying, “Eli, Eli, lima sabachthani?”  That is, “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”
verses.explainer: About three o'clock, Jesus cries out: 'Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani?' -- 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' He quotes Psalm 22:1. This is the most agonizing moment: Jesus experiences the spiritual abandonment that sin causes -- separation from God.

crossReferences.text: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Why are you so far from helping me, and from the words of my groaning?
crossReferences.connection: David wrote this psalm of lament. Jesus takes it as his own on the cross, experiencing the ultimate abandonment. But Psalm 22 ends in vindication and praise -- the cry of desolation is not the final word.

verses.text: Some of them who stood there, when they heard it, said, “This man is calling Elijah.”
verses.explainer: Some standing there hear it and say: this man is calling for Elijah. They misunderstand 'Eli' as a call for the prophet Elijah.

verses.text: Immediately one of them ran, and took a sponge, and filled it with vinegar, and put it on a reed, and gave him a drink.
verses.explainer: One of them runs and fills a sponge with sour wine, puts it on a reed, and offers it to him. A small act of compassion -- or perhaps an attempt to keep him alive longer.

verses.text: The rest said, “Let him be. Let’s see whether Elijah comes to save him.”
verses.explainer: Others say: leave him alone. Let us see whether Elijah comes to save him. They want to watch and see if a miracle happens.

verses.text: Jesus cried again with a loud voice, and yielded up his spirit.
verses.explainer: Jesus cries out again with a loud voice and yields up his spirit. He does not simply die -- he 'yields' his spirit. Even in death, Jesus acts. He gives up his life; it is not taken from him.

verses.text: Behold, the veil of the temple was torn in two from the top to the bottom. The earth quaked and the rocks were split.
verses.explainer: The curtain of the temple is torn in two from top to bottom. The earth shakes, and the rocks are split. The curtain separated the Holy of Holies -- God's inner presence -- from everyone else. It is torn from top to bottom, meaning God did it. The barrier between God and humanity is removed by Jesus' death.

verses.text: The tombs were opened, and many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep were raised;
verses.explainer: Tombs are opened, and many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep are raised. A foretaste of the resurrection -- death's power is broken.

verses.text: and coming out of the tombs after his resurrection, they entered into the holy city and appeared to many.
verses.explainer: Coming out of the tombs after his resurrection, they go into the holy city and appear to many. The resurrected saints are connected to Jesus' own resurrection.

verses.text: Now the centurion, and those who were with him watching Jesus, when they saw the earthquake, and the things that were done, feared exceedingly, saying, “Truly this was the Son of God.”
verses.explainer: The centurion and those keeping watch see the earthquake and everything that has happened. They are terrified and say: truly this was the Son of God! A Roman soldier -- a pagan Gentile -- makes the confession the religious leaders refused to make. Matthew's gospel began with Gentile magi worshiping; it approaches its climax with a Gentile soldier confessing.

verses.text: Many women were there watching from afar, who had followed Jesus from Galilee, serving him.
verses.explainer: Many women are there, watching from a distance. They had followed Jesus from Galilee, serving him.

verses.text: Among them were Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joses, and the mother of the sons of Zebedee.
verses.explainer: Among them are Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joseph, and the mother of Zebedee's sons. The women who followed Jesus remain when the male disciples have fled.

verses.text: When evening had come, a rich man from Arimathaea, named Joseph, who himself was also Jesus’ disciple came.
verses.explainer: When evening comes, a rich man from Arimathea named Joseph -- who was also a disciple of Jesus -- goes to Pilate.

verses.text: This man went to Pilate, and asked for Jesus’ body. Then Pilate commanded the body to be given up.
verses.explainer: He asks for Jesus' body. Pilate orders it given to him. Without Joseph's intervention, Jesus' body would have been thrown into a common grave for criminals.

verses.text: Joseph took the body, and wrapped it in a clean linen cloth,
verses.explainer: Joseph takes the body, wraps it in a clean linen shroud.

verses.text: and laid it in his own new tomb, which he had cut out in the rock, and he rolled a great stone against the door of the tomb, and departed.
verses.explainer: He lays it in his own new tomb, which he had cut in the rock. He rolls a great stone to the entrance and departs. A rich man's tomb for a carpenter's son -- fulfilling Isaiah 53:9, 'his grave was with the rich.'

verses.text: Mary Magdalene was there, and the other Mary, sitting opposite the tomb.
verses.explainer: Mary Magdalene and the other Mary are there, sitting opposite the tomb. The women keep vigil. They will be the first witnesses of the resurrection.

verses.text: Now on the next day, which was the day after the Preparation Day, the chief priests and the Pharisees were gathered together to Pilate,
verses.explainer: The next day (Saturday), the chief priests and Pharisees go to Pilate.

verses.text: saying, “Sir, we remember what that deceiver said while he was still alive: ‘After three days I will rise again.’
verses.explainer: They say: sir, we remember that deceiver said while alive, 'After three days I will rise.' They call Jesus a 'deceiver' -- but they take his prediction seriously enough to act on it.

verses.text: Command therefore that the tomb be made secure until the third day, lest perhaps his disciples come at night and steal him away, and tell the people, ‘He is risen from the dead;’ and the last deception will be worse than the first.”
verses.explainer: Order the tomb to be made secure until the third day, or his disciples may steal the body and tell people he has risen. The last deception would be worse than the first. Their own security measures will become evidence for the resurrection.

verses.text: Pilate said to them, “You have a guard. Go, make it as secure as you can.”
verses.explainer: Pilate says: you have a guard. Go, make it as secure as you can.

verses.text: So they went with the guard and made the tomb secure, sealing the stone.
verses.explainer: They go and make the tomb secure by sealing the stone and setting a guard. The most heavily guarded tomb in history -- sealed, guarded by Roman soldiers. Every precaution taken. And it will all prove useless.

explainer.summary: Early Sunday morning, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary go to the tomb. There is a violent earthquake as an angel descends, rolls back the stone, and sits on it. The guards shake with fear and become like dead men. The angel tells the women: he is not here -- he has risen, just as he said. Come see the empty tomb, then go tell the disciples. As they run to tell the disciples, Jesus himself meets them. The guards report to the chief priests, who bribe them to say the disciples stole the body. The eleven disciples go to Galilee, where Jesus gives the Great Commission: go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them and teaching them. 'I am with you always, to the end of the age.'
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 28 is the climax of the entire gospel. Everything has led to this: the genealogy, the birth, the teaching, the miracles, the cross -- all of it points to and is vindicated by the resurrection. Matthew's gospel began with 'Immanuel -- God with us' (1:23) and ends with Jesus' promise: 'I am with you always' (28:20). The book is framed by the promise of God's presence.

verses.text: Now after the Sabbath, as it began to dawn on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary came to see the tomb.
verses.explainer: After the Sabbath, at dawn on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary go to see the tomb. The women who watched the burial are the first to return. Their devotion outlasts the male disciples' courage.

verses.text: Behold, there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from the sky, and came and rolled away the stone from the door, and sat on it.
verses.explainer: There is a great earthquake. An angel of the Lord descends from heaven, comes and rolls back the stone, and sits on it. The angel does not open the tomb to let Jesus out -- Jesus is already risen. The stone is rolled back so the witnesses can look in and see that it is empty.

verses.text: His appearance was like lightning, and his clothing white as snow.
verses.explainer: The angel's appearance is like lightning and his clothing white as snow. Heavenly messengers are depicted with terrifying radiance.

verses.text: For fear of him, the guards shook, and became like dead men.
verses.explainer: The guards tremble with fear and become like dead men. Roman soldiers -- trained warriors -- are paralyzed with terror. The most powerful military force on earth is rendered helpless.

verses.text: The angel answered the women, “Don’t be afraid, for I know that you seek Jesus, who has been crucified.
verses.explainer: The angel speaks to the women: do not be afraid. I know you are looking for Jesus who was crucified. He addresses their fear first, then their search. They came looking for a dead body.

verses.text: He is not here, for he has risen, just like he said. Come, see the place where the Lord was lying.
verses.explainer: He is not here, for he has risen, as he said. Come, see the place where he lay. The most important announcement in human history, delivered in a single sentence. And note: 'as he said.' Jesus predicted this repeatedly. It should not be a surprise.

verses.text: Go quickly and tell his disciples, ‘He has risen from the dead, and behold, he goes before you into Galilee; there you will see him.’ Behold, I have told you.”
verses.explainer: Go quickly and tell his disciples: he has risen from the dead and is going ahead of you to Galilee. There you will see him. The women are the first commissioned evangelists -- sent to proclaim the resurrection to the other disciples.

verses.text: They departed quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, and ran to bring his disciples word.
verses.explainer: They leave the tomb quickly with fear and great joy, and run to tell his disciples. Fear and joy together -- the proper response to an encounter with the living God.

verses.text: As they went to tell his disciples, behold, Jesus met them, saying, “Rejoice!” They came and took hold of his feet, and worshiped him.
verses.explainer: And behold, Jesus meets them and says: 'Greetings!' They come and take hold of his feet and worship him. He is physical, touchable, real. This is not a ghost or a vision. They grasp his feet -- the same feet that walked on water, that were nailed to the cross.

verses.text: Then Jesus said to them, “Don’t be afraid. Go tell my brothers  that they should go into Galilee, and there they will see me.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: do not be afraid. Go and tell my brothers to go to Galilee, and there they will see me. He calls the disciples 'brothers' -- even after they abandoned him. No rebuke, no shame. Just a reunion invitation.

verses.text: Now while they were going, behold, some of the guards came into the city, and told the chief priests all the things that had happened.
verses.explainer: While they are going, some of the guard go into the city and report to the chief priests everything that happened. The guards are witnesses too.

verses.text: When they were assembled with the elders, and had taken counsel, they gave a large amount of silver to the soldiers,
verses.explainer: The chief priests meet with the elders and devise a plan. They give the soldiers a large sum of money.

verses.text: saying, “Say that his disciples came by night, and stole him away while we slept.
verses.explainer: They instruct them: say, 'His disciples came during the night and stole him away while we were sleeping.' The cover story is absurd -- sleeping guards cannot know what happened, and Roman soldiers who fell asleep on duty faced execution.

verses.text: If this comes to the governor’s ears, we will persuade him and make you free of worry.”
verses.explainer: If this comes to the governor's ears, we will satisfy him and keep you out of trouble. They promise to use their political connections to protect the soldiers from punishment.

verses.text: So they took the money and did as they were told. This saying was spread abroad among the Jews, and continues until today.
verses.explainer: The soldiers take the money and do as instructed. This story has been spread among the Jews to this day. Matthew notes that this false narrative was still circulating when he wrote.

verses.text: But the eleven disciples went into Galilee, to the mountain where Jesus had sent them.
verses.explainer: The eleven disciples go to Galilee, to the mountain Jesus had designated. Back where it all started -- Galilee, where Jesus began his ministry.

verses.text: When they saw him, they bowed down to him, but some doubted.
verses.explainer: When they see him, they worship him -- but some doubted. Even in the presence of the risen Jesus, some struggle with doubt. Matthew's honesty is remarkable. Faith and doubt coexist.

verses.text: Jesus came to them and spoke to them, saying, “All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth.
verses.explainer: Jesus comes and says: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. The foundation of the Great Commission is not their ability but his authority. Everything that follows rests on this claim.

verses.text: Go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,
verses.explainer: Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit. The mission expands from Israel to all nations. The Trinitarian baptismal formula reveals the full nature of God: Father, Son, and Spirit.

verses.text: teaching them to observe all things that I commanded you. Behold, I am with you always, even to the end of the age.” Amen.
verses.explainer: Teaching them to observe everything I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age. The gospel's final words echo its first promise: Immanuel, God with us. The story began with God entering human life through a baby in Bethlehem. It ends with God promising to never leave. The presence that started in a manger continues to the end of time.

thenVsNow.then: Eleven ordinary men from Galilee were commissioned to reach every nation on earth. They had no armies, no money, no political power. They had only Jesus' authority and his promise to be with them.
thenVsNow.now: The movement that began with these eleven has reached every continent and every major people group on earth. Two billion people identify as Christian. The Great Commission is still being fulfilled, and the promise still holds: I am with you always.
